title: Project Lifecycles: the loop you ship into

questions.question: What's wrong with treating 'done' as 'shipped'?
options: Shipping is the loop's midpoint; operate, monitor, and improve are stages inside the loop
Nothing — once code is in production the project is genuinely complete
Shipping only counts as done for AI systems, not for classic software
'Done' should mean code-complete, measured before any testing or release
questions.explanation: Real systems are loops, not lines: release is the midpoint, and most of a system's lifetime cost lives in operate/monitor/improve. The loop only 'closes' by feeding learning back into the next iteration.

questions.question: A deployed fraud model nobody has touched in six months starts missing fraud. No code changed. What most likely happened?
options: A dependency upgrade silently broke the model's code path
The world changed — data/concept drift moved fraud patterns under a static model
The model was never actually deployed to production
Random model non-determinism caused a temporary dip
questions.explanation: AI/ML systems can fail when the world changes and you change nothing — fraud patterns (the data) drifted under a static model. Monitoring should catch the quality drop; continuous training closes the loop.

questions.question: What fundamentally distinguishes how classic software fails from how AI/ML systems fail?
options: AI/ML systems never fail once they pass their initial evaluation
Classic software fails only in production, while AI/ML fails only in training
Classic software fails when you change it; AI/ML can fail when the world changes and you change nothing
Classic software fails on data drift, while AI/ML fails on code bugs
questions.explanation: This is why monitoring and retraining are mandatory loop stages for AI/ML, not optional ops: the world (data distribution or the input→output rule) can move under a static model. The distractors invert or fabricate the failure modes.

questions.question: What did Agile change about the software lifecycle?
options: It removed the testing stage in favor of continuous deployment
It made the lifecycle iterative — small increments, welcoming changing requirements
It merged the development and operations teams into one role
It replaced human requirements with automatically generated specs
questions.explanation: Agile made the loop iterative ('welcome changing requirements, even late'). Fusing dev and ops is DevOps, not Agile; Agile didn't remove testing or automate requirements away.

questions.question: Why do modern teams draw Release and Deploy as two separate stages?
options: Deploy is only for staging environments; Release is only for production
They are the same thing; the split is purely historical
Deploy = code running in prod; Release = users exposed to it — decoupling them (flags/canary/blue-green) enables safe on-demand shipping and clean rollback
Release always happens before deploy in the pipeline order
questions.explanation: Decoupling deploy from release is what lets elite teams ship safely and roll back by flipping a flag or switching the router. The catch is state — schema changes can't be flipped, so migrations are sequenced (expand→migrate→contract).

questions.question: A team says '100% uptime is our goal.' What would an SRE say?
options: Agree — 100% is the only acceptable reliability target
100% is the wrong target; pick an SLO below 100% so the error budget can fund feature velocity
Uptime doesn't matter as long as deploys are frequent
100% is fine, but only if it's measured monthly rather than continuously
questions.explanation: SRE deliberately targets below 100%; the resulting error budget is what funds velocity. Spend none of it and you're shipping too cautiously. The measurement-cadence and deploy-frequency distractors miss the point.

questions.question: Since you can't assertEqual a non-deterministic LLM, what plays the role of the correctness oracle?
options: Lowering the temperature until outputs become deterministic
Swapping in a larger model that is right more often
Evals — the heartbeat that tells you whether a change is an improvement or a regression
The detailed instructions written in the system prompt
questions.explanation: Evals are the oracle for a non-deterministic system; without a harness you literally can't tell whether a change helped. Temperature, model size, and prompt wording don't give you a measurable verdict.

questions.question: Which two properties does an LLM app add that the classic software lifecycle never had to model?
options: Version control and continuous integration
Non-determinism and a data feedback loop (production traces become the next eval set)
Load balancing and horizontal scaling
Static typing and compile-time checks
questions.explanation: Non-determinism means you can't unit-test with assertEqual, and the data feedback loop makes trace-mining a first-class stage. The other options are ordinary software concerns that predate LLM apps.

questions.question: Your LLM-as-judge agrees with your gold set 92% of the time. Is it safe to trust it blindly?
options: Yes — above 90% agreement means the judge is reliable
Yes, as long as you used a larger model as the judge
No — judges carry position/verbosity/self-enhancement bias and need ongoing validation against human labels; offline agreement is not online ground truth
No — judges are never useful and should be replaced entirely by code assertions
questions.explanation: An unvalidated judge launders vibes into fake rigor; it needs its own continuing eval against fresh human labels. Judges ARE useful (so the last option overcorrects), and a bigger model doesn't remove the bias.

questions.question: Per the data-centric view, where should you look first to improve a model's accuracy?
options: The choice of algorithm and its hyperparameters
The data — quality, labeling consistency, and train/serve feature parity
A bigger GPU so you can train for longer
The model's random seed
questions.explanation: The trained model is the small black box; most leverage lives in the data and feature pipelines around it. Better data usually beats a better algorithm in practice.

questions.question: Live accuracy drops 8 points overnight. Why is 'just retrain on fresh data' the wrong default first move?
options: Retraining is always too expensive to be worth considering
The cause may be a broken pipeline, training-serving skew, or a feedback loop — diagnose the failure type first; blind retraining on contaminated data can accelerate decay
Fresh data is never available quickly enough to retrain
You should always roll back the model instead of ever retraining
questions.explanation: Different causes (skew, pipeline breakage, feedback loops, label drift) need different fixes; retraining on drifted or feedback-contaminated data can make decay worse. The absolutes ('always too expensive', 'never available', 'always roll back') are wrong.

questions.question: What is the difference between data drift and concept drift?
options: Data drift changes the model's code; concept drift changes its weights
Data drift is the input distribution P(X) shifting; concept drift is the input→output relationship P(Y|X) shifting
They are two names for the same phenomenon
Data drift only affects training, while concept drift only affects serving
questions.explanation: Data drift = the inputs move (P(X)); concept drift = the rule mapping inputs to outputs moves (P(Y|X)). They need different detection (distribution proxies vs. live accuracy) and different responses, which is why 'accuracy dropped → retrain' is too blunt.
